query: Compare potential real-time revenues for a 100 MW solar PV farm in Abilene Texas (with ERCOT West Hub prices) vs Houston (ERCOT Houston Hub) based on 2023 data. Assume the solar farms have double-axis tracking panels and no curtailments associated with the farms. You can assume overall system loss for the farms stands at 10%
final_answer:
---

# Comparison: 100 MW Solar PV Farm — Abilene (ERCOT West Hub) vs. Houston (ERCOT Houston Hub), 2023

## Setup & Assumptions

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| System Capacity | 100 MW |
| Tracking | Double-axis (2-axis) |
| System Loss | 10% |
| Curtailment | None assumed |
| Price Data | ERCOT Real-Time LMPs (15-min SCED, averaged to hourly) |
| Solar Generation | Renewables.ninja PV model (double-axis tracking, 10% system loss) |
| Year | 2023 (Jan 1 – Dec 31) |

**Sources:**
- Solar profiles: [Renewables.ninja](https://www.renewables.ninja/) PV generation model for Abilene, TX (32.45°N, 99.75°W) and Houston, TX (29.76°N, 95.37°W)
- Real-time electricity prices: ERCOT LMP data from the `ercot_db.ercot_lmp` table (zone_name = 'west' and 'houston', is_day_ahead = false)

---

## Headline Results

| Metric | Abilene (West Hub) | Houston (Houston Hub) | Difference |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| **Annual Generation (MWh)** | 219,720 | 193,193 | +26,527 (Abilene +13.7%) |
| **Capacity Factor** | 25.08% | 22.05% | +3.03 pp |
| **Annual Real-Time Revenue** | **$22,085,152** | **$19,768,976** | **+$2,316,176 (Abilene +10.5%)** |
| **Revenue per MW** | $220,852 | $197,690 | +$23,162 |
| **Weighted Avg Price Received ($/MWh)** | $100.52 | $102.33 | –$1.81 (Houston slightly higher) |
| **Simple Avg RT LMP – All Hours ($/MWh)** | $54.72 | $50.01 | +$4.71 |
| **Hours with Negative Prices During Generation** | 328 | 28 | +300 (Abilene far more) |

---

## Key Takeaways

### 1. Abilene Wins on Revenue Despite Lower Prices
Abilene generates **$2.3 million more** in annual real-time revenue (+10.5%) than Houston. This is driven entirely by **higher energy output** — Abilene's superior solar resource (more sunny days, higher irradiance in West Texas) yields 13.7% more generation (219,720 MWh vs. 193,193 MWh). Even though Houston receives a marginally higher weighted-average price ($102.33/MWh vs. $100.52/MWh), the generation advantage more than compensates.

### 2. Houston Has Better Price Capture
Houston's weighted-average price received during solar hours ($102.33/MWh) is slightly higher than Abilene's ($100.52/MWh). This is because the **West Hub experiences far more negative price hours during solar generation** (328 hours vs. only 28 in Houston), reflecting the heavy solar congestion in West Texas. Houston's load center proximity means less solar-driven price suppression.

### 3. August Dominates Revenue for Both Locations
August 2023 was an extraordinary revenue month due to extreme heat and price spikes across ERCOT:

| Month | Abilene Revenue | Houston Revenue | Abilene Gen (MWh) | Houston Gen (MWh) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Jan | $505,862 | $397,219 | 16,385 | 14,253 |
| Feb | $316,114 | $279,904 | 14,809 | 14,071 |
| Mar | $803,118 | $598,156 | 19,001 | 16,135 |
| Apr | $727,754 | $441,330 | 20,842 | 15,870 |
| May | $872,604 | $662,183 | 16,213 | 15,231 |
| Jun | $2,460,516 | $2,136,575 | 20,017 | 17,902 |
| Jul | $1,541,319 | $1,479,847 | 21,922 | 19,108 |
| **Aug** | **$8,959,184** | **$8,836,770** | 21,661 | 18,642 |
| Sep | $3,650,657 | $3,370,817 | 18,317 | 16,988 |
| Oct | $831,567 | $707,812 | 16,811 | 16,435 |
| Nov | $736,671 | $497,833 | 16,568 | 13,541 |
| Dec | $679,784 | $360,529 | 17,173 | 15,017 |

August alone accounted for **40.6%** of Abilene's annual revenue and **44.7%** of Houston's. This reflects the extreme pricing events during the August 2023 heat wave, when ERCOT real-time prices spiked above $5,000/MWh.

### 4. Revenue by Price Tier

| Price Tier ($/MWh) | Abilene Revenue | Abilene Gen (MWh) | Houston Revenue | Houston Gen (MWh) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| < $0 (negative) | –$58,281 | 15,056 | –$4,294 | 1,092 |
| $0–25 | $1,034,619 | 71,660 | $1,501,773 | 82,260 |
| $25–50 | $2,400,346 | 68,065 | $2,201,313 | 66,037 |
| $50–100 | $2,703,597 | 38,918 | $1,580,625 | 23,473 |
| $100–250 | $2,072,321 | 14,567 | $1,520,888 | 10,287 |
| $250–1,000 | $3,636,858 | 7,150 | $2,987,686 | 5,827 |
| >$1,000 | $10,295,692 | 4,304 | $9,980,984 | 4,217 |

The **>$1,000/MWh tier** alone generated ~$10 million for both locations — nearly half of total annual revenue — from just ~4,300 MWh of generation during extreme price events. Abilene lost $58,281 to negative pricing (328 hours), while Houston lost only $4,294 (28 hours).

---

## Conclusion

A 100 MW double-axis tracking solar farm in **Abilene, TX (ERCOT West Hub)** would have earned approximately **$22.1 million** in real-time energy revenue in 2023, compared to **$19.8 million** for an identical farm in **Houston, TX (ERCOT Houston Hub)** — a **$2.3 million (10.5%) advantage** for Abilene. The revenue advantage is entirely driven by Abilene's superior solar resource (25.1% vs. 22.1% capacity factor), which more than offsets the West Hub's greater exposure to negative pricing during solar hours. However, Houston offers slightly better price capture per MWh and far fewer negative-price hours, suggesting that if curtailment or congestion were factored in, the gap could narrow. Both locations are heavily dependent on extreme pricing events — August 2023 alone contributed over 40% of annual revenue for both sites.

3. **Get ERCOT real-time prices** for West Hub and Houston Hub for 2023
4. **Calculate and compare revenues**

The user wants to compare potential real-time revenues for a 100 MW solar PV farm in two locations in Texas:
1. Abilene, TX - using ERCOT West Hub prices
2. Houston, TX - using ERCOT Houston Hub prices

I need to:
1. Get solar generation profiles for both locations (double-axis tracking, 100 MW capacity, 10% system loss) for 2023
2. Get ERCOT West Hub and Houston Hub real-time prices for 2023
3. Calculate revenues by multiplying hourly generation by hourly prices for each location

- Checking what ERCOT data is available in the database

Let me do these in parallel.

- Abilene: lat 32.44645, lon -99.7475905
- Houston: lat 29.7589382, lon -95.3676974

Now I need to:
1. Get solar profiles for both locations for 2023 (double-axis tracking = tracking type 2, system_loss = 0.1, capacity = 100 kW since the API normalizes to capacity factor)

Let me first check what zones are available and then get the solar profiles.

tool_input.query: SELECT DISTINCT zone_name FROM ercot_lmp WHERE zone_type = 'hub' ORDER BY zone_name

steps.content: I have the solar profiles saved and confirmed the hub names. Now let me pull the real-time LMP data for both the West Hub and Houston Hub for 2023.

tool_input.query: SELECT timestamp, lmp FROM ercot_lmp WHERE zone_name = 'west' AND is_day_ahead = false AND timestamp >= '2023-01-01' AND timestamp < '2024-01-01' ORDER BY timestamp
SELECT timestamp, lmp FROM ercot_lmp WHERE zone_name = 'houston' AND is_day_ahead = false AND timestamp >= '2023-01-01' AND timestamp < '2024-01-01' ORDER BY timestamp

steps.content: I have all the data. The ERCOT prices are at 15-minute intervals with some duplicates. Now let me process everything in Python to calculate and compare revenues.
steps.reasoning:
I see the ERCOT LMP data is at 15-minute intervals (70080 rows = 8760 hours * 4 intervals * 2 duplicates? Wait, 70080 / 8760 = 8. That's odd. Let me check - 8760 hours * 4 (15-min intervals) = 35040. But we have 70080, which is 35040 * 2. There seem to be duplicates. Let me check the data more carefully.

Actually, looking at the preview, the first two rows have the same timestamp "2023-01-01 00:00:00" and the same lmp "-2.56". So there are duplicates. Let me handle this in Python.

The solar profiles are hourly (8760 rows). I need to:
1. Load the solar profiles (hourly)
2. Load the LMP data (15-min, with duplicates)
3. Clean the LMP data (remove duplicates, average to hourly)
4. Match solar generation with prices
5. Calculate revenue = generation (MWh) * price ($/MWh) for each hour
6. Sum up annual revenue for both locations

Let me write Python code to do all of this.

tool_input.code:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Load solar profiles (hourly, 8760 rows)
solar_abilene = pd.read_csv('/work/solar_profile_20260616_150310.csv')
solar_houston = pd.read_csv('/work/solar_profile_20260616_150312.csv')

print("=== Solar Profile Data ===")
print(f"Abilene shape: {solar_abilene.shape}")
print(f"Houston shape: {solar_houston.shape}")
print(f"Abilene columns: {solar_abilene.columns.tolist()}")
print(f"Abilene head:\n{solar_abilene.head()}")
print(f"Abilene tail:\n{solar_abilene.tail()}")
print(f"Abilene electricity stats:\n{solar_abilene['electricity'].describe()}")
print(f"Houston electricity stats:\n{solar_houston['electricity'].describe()}")

# Load ERCOT LMP data
west_lmp = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_db_20260616_150322.csv')
houston_lmp = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_db_20260616_150323.csv')

print("\n=== ERCOT LMP Data ===")
print(f"West LMP shape: {west_lmp.shape}")
print(f"Houston LMP shape: {houston_lmp.shape}")
print(f"West LMP columns: {west_lmp.columns.tolist()}")
print(f"West LMP head:\n{west_lmp.head(10)}")
print(f"West LMP timestamp range: {west_lmp['timestamp'].min()} to {west_lmp['timestamp'].max()}")

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Load solar profiles (hourly, 8760 rows)
solar_abilene = pd.read_csv('/work/solar_profile_20260616_150310.csv')
solar_houston = pd.read_csv('/work/solar_profile_20260616_150312.csv')

# Load ERCOT LMP data (15-min intervals, with duplicates)
west_lmp = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_db_20260616_150322.csv')
houston_lmp = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_db_20260616_150323.csv')

# The LMP data has 70080 rows for 2023. 8760 hours * 4 intervals = 35040.
# 70080 = 35040 * 2, so there are duplicates. Let's deduplicate.
west_lmp = west_lmp.drop_duplicates().reset_index(drop=True)
houston_lmp = houston_lmp.drop_duplicates().reset_index(drop=True)

print(f"West LMP after dedup: {west_lmp.shape}")
print(f"Houston LMP after dedup: {houston_lmp.shape}")

# Convert timestamps
west_lmp['timestamp'] = pd.to_datetime(west_lmp['timestamp'])
houston_lmp['timestamp'] = pd.to_datetime(houston_lmp['timestamp'])

# Sort by timestamp
west_lmp = west_lmp.sort_values('timestamp').reset_index(drop=True)
houston_lmp = houston_lmp.sort_values('timestamp').reset_index(drop=True)

print(f"West LMP range: {west_lmp['timestamp'].min()} to {west_lmp['timestamp'].max()}")
print(f"West LMP count: {len(west_lmp)}")
print(f"Expected 15-min intervals for 2023: {365*24*4} = {365*24*4}")

# Now we need to convert 15-min LMP to hourly average to match solar profiles
# Add an hour column by flooring to hour
west_lmp['hour'] = west_lmp['timestamp'].dt.floor('h')
houston_lmp['hour'] = houston_lmp['timestamp'].dt.floor('h')

# Group by hour and take mean of LMP
west_hourly = west_lmp.groupby('hour')['lmp'].mean().reset_index()
west_hourly.columns = ['timestamp', 'lmp']
houston_hourly = houston_lmp.groupby('hour')['lmp'].mean().reset_index()
houston_hourly.columns = ['timestamp', 'lmp']

print(f"\nWest hourly LMP count: {len(west_hourly)}")
print(f"Houston hourly LMP count: {len(houston_hourly)}")
print(f"West hourly LMP stats:\n{west_hourly['lmp'].describe()}")
print(f"Houston hourly LMP stats:\n{houston_hourly['lmp'].describe()}")

# Now prepare solar data - convert time to datetime
solar_abilene['time'] = pd.to_datetime(solar_abilene['time'])
solar_houston['time'] = pd.to_datetime(solar_houston['time'])

# The solar 'electricity' column is in kW (since capacity=100 kW was passed... wait, capacity=100 means 100 kW)
# Actually, looking at the data, max is 90.0 for Abilene and 88.285 for Houston
# With 100 kW capacity and 10% system loss, max output would be 90 kW. So electricity is in kW.
# But we want 100 MW = 100,000 kW. So we need to scale by 100,000/100 = 1,000
# Actually, the API returns output in kW for the given capacity in kW.
# We passed capacity=100 (kW), so the output is in kW.
# To get 100 MW = 100,000 kW, multiply by 100,000/100 = 1,000

# Actually, let me reconsider. The renewables.ninja API returns electricity in kW.
# We passed capacity=100 (kW). The max output is 90 kW (100 * 0.9 after 10% loss).
# For a 100 MW farm, we need to scale: 100 MW = 100,000 kW
# Scale factor = 100,000 / 100 = 1,000

scale_factor = 100000 / 100  # Convert from 100 kW to 100 MW (100,000 kW)

solar_abilene['output_mw'] = solar_abilene['electricity'] * scale_factor / 1000  # kW to MW
solar_houston['output_mw'] = solar_houston['electricity'] * scale_factor / 1000

print(f"\nAbilene solar output (MW) stats:\n{solar_abilene['output_mw'].describe()}")
print(f"Houston solar output (MW) stats:\n{solar_houston['output_mw'].describe()}")

# Total annual generation in MWh
abilene_total_mwh = solar_abilene['output_mw'].sum()
houston_total_mwh = solar_houston['output_mw'].sum()
print(f"\nAbilene total annual generation: {abilene_total_mwh:,.1f} MWh")
print(f"Houston total annual generation: {houston_total_mwh:,.1f} MWh")
print(f"Abilene capacity factor: {abilene_total_mwh / (100 * 8760) * 100:.2f}%")
print(f"Houston capacity factor: {houston_total_mwh / (100 * 8760) * 100:.2f}%")

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Load all data
solar_abilene = pd.read_csv('/work/solar_profile_20260616_150310.csv')
solar_houston = pd.read_csv('/work/solar_profile_20260616_150312.csv')
west_lmp = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_db_20260616_150322.csv')
houston_lmp = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_db_20260616_150323.csv')

# Deduplicate LMP data
west_lmp = west_lmp.drop_duplicates().reset_index(drop=True)
houston_lmp = houston_lmp.drop_duplicates().reset_index(drop=True)

# Convert timestamps
west_lmp['timestamp'] = pd.to_datetime(west_lmp['timestamp'])
houston_lmp['timestamp'] = pd.to_datetime(houston_lmp['timestamp'])

# Sort
west_lmp = west_lmp.sort_values('timestamp').reset_index(drop=True)
houston_lmp = houston_lmp.sort_values('timestamp').reset_index(drop=True)

# Convert to hourly average
west_lmp['hour'] = west_lmp['timestamp'].dt.floor('h')
houston_lmp['hour'] = houston_lmp['timestamp'].dt.floor('h')
west_hourly = west_lmp.groupby('hour')['lmp'].mean().reset_index()
west_hourly.columns = ['timestamp', 'lmp']
houston_hourly = houston_lmp.groupby('hour')['lmp'].mean().reset_index()
houston_hourly.columns = ['timestamp', 'lmp']

# Prepare solar data
solar_abilene['time'] = pd.to_datetime(solar_abilene['time'])
solar_houston['time'] = pd.to_datetime(solar_houston['time'])

# Scale to 100 MW (API used capacity=100 kW, so scale by 1000 to get kW, then /1000 for MW = just use as MW directly)
# Actually: capacity=100 kW, output in kW. For 100 MW = 100,000 kW, scale = 1000
# output_mw = electricity_kW * 1000 / 1000 = electricity (the numbers already look like MW for 100 MW plant)
# Wait: max electricity = 90.0. If capacity=100 kW with 10% loss, max = 90 kW.
# For 100 MW plant: 90 MW max. So output_mw = electricity * (100 MW / 100 kW) = electricity * 1000 kW / 1000 = electricity * 1
# Hmm, that gives 90 MW max which is correct for 100 MW with 10% loss!
# Actually no: electricity is in kW. 90 kW for a 100 kW system.
# For 100 MW system: 90 MW. So output_mw = electricity_kW * (100,000 kW / 100 kW) / 1000 = electricity * 1000 / 1000 = electricity
# So the numbers ARE already in MW for a 100 MW plant! The scale factor is exactly 1.

# Let me verify: 100 kW capacity, 10% loss → max 90 kW output
# For 100 MW: max 90 MW. The electricity column shows max 90.0. 
# So electricity * (100,000/100) = electricity * 1000 kW = electricity MW (after /1000)
# output_mw = electricity * 1000 / 1000 = electricity. Yes, it's already MW!

solar_abilene['output_mw'] = solar_abilene['electricity']  # Already in MW for 100 MW plant
solar_houston['output_mw'] = solar_houston['electricity']

# Merge solar with LMP data
# Solar timestamps are at hour start (00:00, 01:00, etc.)
# LMP hourly timestamps are also at hour start
# But solar has 8760 rows, LMP has 8759 rows (one missing hour)

# Let's check which hour is missing in LMP
all_hours_2023 = pd.date_range('2023-01-01 00:00:00', '2023-12-31 23:00:00', freq='h')
print(f"Total hours in 2023: {len(all_hours_2023)}")
missing_west = set(all_hours_2023) - set(west_hourly['timestamp'])
missing_houston = set(all_hours_2023) - set(houston_hourly['timestamp'])
print(f"Missing hours in West: {missing_west}")
print(f"Missing hours in Houston: {missing_houston}")

# Merge for Abilene (West Hub)
abilene_merged = pd.merge(solar_abilene[['time', 'output_mw']], west_hourly, 
                          left_on='time', right_on='timestamp', how='left')
abilene_merged['lmp'] = abilene_merged['lmp'].fillna(0)  # Fill missing with 0

# Merge for Houston (Houston Hub)
houston_merged = pd.merge(solar_houston[['time', 'output_mw']], houston_hourly,
                          left_on='time', right_on='timestamp', how='left')
houston_merged['lmp'] = houston_merged['lmp'].fillna(0)

# Calculate hourly revenue: output (MW) * price ($/MWh) * 1 hour = $
abilene_merged['revenue'] = abilene_merged['output_mw'] * abilene_merged['lmp']
houston_merged['revenue'] = houston_merged['output_mw'] * houston_merged['lmp']

# Annual totals
abilene_total_revenue = abilene_merged['revenue'].sum()
houston_total_revenue = houston_merged['revenue'].sum()
abilene_total_mwh = abilene_merged['output_mw'].sum()
houston_total_mwh = houston_merged['output_mw'].sum()

# Weighted average price received (only during generation hours)
abilene_gen_hours = abilene_merged[abilene_merged['output_mw'] > 0]
houston_gen_hours = houston_merged[houston_merged['output_mw'] > 0]
abilene_wa_price = abilene_gen_hours['revenue'].sum() / abilene_gen_hours['output_mw'].sum()
houston_wa_price = houston_gen_hours['revenue'].sum() / houston_gen_hours['output_mw'].sum()

# Monthly breakdown
abilene_merged['month'] = abilene_merged['time'].dt.month
houston_merged['month'] = houston_merged['time'].dt.month

abilene_monthly = abilene_merged.groupby('month').agg(
    revenue=('revenue', 'sum'),
    generation=('output_mw', 'sum'),
    avg_price=('lmp', 'mean')
).reset_index()

houston_monthly = houston_merged.groupby('month').agg(
    revenue=('revenue', 'sum'),
    generation=('output_mw', 'sum'),
    avg_price=('lmp', 'mean')
).reset_index()

# Print comprehensive results
print("\n" + "="*80)
print("COMPREHENSIVE COMPARISON: 100 MW Solar PV Farm - Abilene vs Houston (2023)")
print("="*80)

print(f"\n{'Metric':<45} {'Abilene (West Hub)':>20} {'Houston (Houston Hub)':>20}")
print("-"*85)
print(f"{'System Capacity':<45} {'100 MW':>20} {'100 MW':>20}")
print(f"{'Tracking Type':<45} {'2-axis':>20} {'2-axis':>20}")
print(f"{'System Loss':<45} {'10%':>20} {'10%':>20}")
print(f"{'Annual Generation (MWh)':<45} {abilene_total_mwh:>20,.0f} {houston_total_mwh:>20,.0f}")
print(f"{'Capacity Factor':<45} {abilene_total_mwh/(100*8760)*100:>19.2f}% {houston_total_mwh/(100*8760)*100:>19.2f}%")
print(f"{'Annual Real-Time Revenue':<45} {'$'+f'{abilene_total_revenue:,.0f}':>20} {'$'+f'{houston_total_revenue:,.0f}':>20}")
print(f"{'Revenue per MW':<45} {'$'+f'{abilene_total_revenue/100:,.0f}':>20} {'$'+f'{houston_total_revenue/100:,.0f}':>20}")
print(f"{'Weighted Avg Price Received ($/MWh)':<45} {'$'+f'{abilene_wa_price:,.2f}':>20} {'$'+f'{houston_wa_price:,.2f}':>20}")
print(f"{'Simple Avg RT LMP - All Hours ($/MWh)':<45} {'$'+f'{abilene_merged[\"lmp\"].mean():,.2f}':>20} {'$'+f'{houston_merged[\"lmp\"].mean():,.2f}':>20}")
print(f"{'Difference in Revenue':<45} {'':>20} {'$'+f'{houston_total_revenue - abilene_total_revenue:,.0f}':>20}")
print(f"{'% Difference':<45} {'':>20} {f'{(houston_total_revenue - abilene_total_revenue)/abilene_total_revenue*100:,.1f}%':>20}")

print(f"\n{'Monthly Breakdown':}")
print(f"\n{'Month':<8} {'Abilene Rev ($)':>18} {'Houston Rev ($)':>18} {'Abilene Gen (MWh)':>20} {'Houston Gen (MWh)':>20} {'Abilene Avg LMP':>16} {'Houston Avg LMP':>16}")
print("-"*100)
months = ['Jan','Feb','Mar','Apr','May','Jun','Jul','Aug','Sep','Oct','Nov','Dec']
for i, row in abilene_monthly.iterrows():
    h_row = houston_monthly.iloc[i]
    print(f"{months[i]:<8} {row['revenue']:>18,.0f} {h_row['revenue']:>18,.0f} {row['generation']:>20,.0f} {h_row['generation']:>20,.0f} {row['avg_price']:>16,.2f} {h_row['avg_price']:>16,.2f}")

# Additional stats
print(f"\n{'Additional Statistics':}")
print(f"{'Max hourly revenue - Abilene':<45} {'$'+f'{abilene_merged[\"revenue\"].max():,.0f}':>20}")
print(f"{'Max hourly revenue - Houston':<45} {'$'+f'{houston_merged[\"revenue\"].max():,.0f}':>20}")
print(f"{'Hours with generation - Abilene':<45} {len(abilene_gen_hours):>20}")
print(f"{'Hours with generation - Houston':<45} {len(houston_gen_hours):>20}")
print(f"{'Hours with negative price during gen - Abilene':<45} {len(abilene_gen_hours[abilene_gen_hours['lmp']<0]):>20}")
print(f"{'Hours with negative price during gen - Houston':<45} {len(houston_gen_hours[houston_gen_hours['lmp']<0]):>20}")

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Load all data
solar_abilene = pd.read_csv('/work/solar_profile_20260616_150310.csv')
solar_houston = pd.read_csv('/work/solar_profile_20260616_150312.csv')
west_lmp = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_db_20260616_150322.csv')
houston_lmp = pd.read_csv('/work/ercot_db_20260616_150323.csv')

# Deduplicate LMP data
west_lmp = west_lmp.drop_duplicates().reset_index(drop=True)
houston_lmp = houston_lmp.drop_duplicates().reset_index(drop=True)

# Convert timestamps
west_lmp['timestamp'] = pd.to_datetime(west_lmp['timestamp'])
houston_lmp['timestamp'] = pd.to_datetime(houston_lmp['timestamp'])

# Sort
west_lmp = west_lmp.sort_values('timestamp').reset_index(drop=True)
houston_lmp = houston_lmp.sort_values('timestamp').reset_index(drop=True)

# Convert to hourly average
west_lmp['hour'] = west_lmp['timestamp'].dt.floor('h')
houston_lmp['hour'] = houston_lmp['timestamp'].dt.floor('h')
west_hourly = west_lmp.groupby('hour')['lmp'].mean().reset_index()
west_hourly.columns = ['timestamp', 'lmp']
houston_hourly = houston_lmp.groupby('hour')['lmp'].mean().reset_index()
houston_hourly.columns = ['timestamp', 'lmp']

# Prepare solar data
solar_abilene['time'] = pd.to_datetime(solar_abilene['time'])
solar_houston['time'] = pd.to_datetime(solar_houston['time'])

# The electricity column is already in MW for a 100 MW plant
# (capacity=100 kW passed, output in kW, scale by 1000 to get 100 MW plant in kW, divide by 1000 for MW = same number)
solar_abilene['output_mw'] = solar_abilene['electricity']
solar_houston['output_mw'] = solar_houston['electricity']

# Merge solar with LMP data
abilene_merged = pd.merge(solar_abilene[['time', 'output_mw']], west_hourly, 
                          left_on='time', right_on='timestamp', how='left')
abilene_merged['lmp'] = abilene_merged['lmp'].fillna(0)

houston_merged = pd.merge(solar_houston[['time', 'output_mw']], houston_hourly,
                          left_on='time', right_on='timestamp', how='left')
houston_merged['lmp'] = houston_merged['lmp'].fillna(0)

# Calculate hourly revenue: output (MW) * price ($/MWh) * 1 hour = $
abilene_merged['revenue'] = abilene_merged['output_mw'] * abilene_merged['lmp']
houston_merged['revenue'] = houston_merged['output_mw'] * houston_merged['lmp']

# Annual totals
abilene_total_revenue = abilene_merged['revenue'].sum()
houston_total_revenue = houston_merged['revenue'].sum()
abilene_total_mwh = abilene_merged['output_mw'].sum()
houston_total_mwh = houston_merged['output_mw'].sum()

# Weighted average price received (only during generation hours)
abilene_gen_hours = abilene_merged[abilene_merged['output_mw'] > 0]
houston_gen_hours = houston_merged[houston_merged['output_mw'] > 0]
abilene_wa_price = abilene_gen_hours['revenue'].sum() / abilene_gen_hours['output_mw'].sum()
houston_wa_price = houston_gen_hours['revenue'].sum() / houston_gen_hours['output_mw'].sum()

# Monthly breakdown
abilene_merged['month'] = abilene_merged['time'].dt.month
houston_merged['month'] = houston_merged['time'].dt.month

abilene_monthly = abilene_merged.groupby('month').agg(
    revenue=('revenue', 'sum'),
    generation=('output_mw', 'sum'),
    avg_price=('lmp', 'mean')
).reset_index()

houston_monthly = houston_merged.groupby('month').agg(
    revenue=('revenue', 'sum'),
    generation=('output_mw', 'sum'),
    avg_price=('lmp', 'mean')
).reset_index()

# Print comprehensive results
print("=" * 80)
print("COMPREHENSIVE COMPARISON: 100 MW Solar PV Farm - Abilene vs Houston (2023)")
print("=" * 80)

print(f"\n{'Metric':<45} {'Abilene (West)':>20} {'Houston (Houston)':>20}")
print("-" * 85)
print(f"{'System Capacity':<45} {'100 MW':>20} {'100 MW':>20}")
print(f"{'Tracking Type':<45} {'2-axis':>20} {'2-axis':>20}")
print(f"{'System Loss':<45} {'10%':>20} {'10%':>20}")
print(f"{'Annual Generation (MWh)':<45} {abilene_total_mwh:>20,.0f} {houston_total_mwh:>20,.0f}")
print(f"{'Capacity Factor':<45} {abilene_total_mwh/(100*8760)*100:>19.2f}% {houston_total_mwh/(100*8760)*100:>19.2f}%")
print(f"{'Annual Real-Time Revenue':<45} {'$' + format(abilene_total_revenue, ',.0f'):>20} {'$' + format(houston_total_revenue, ',.0f'):>20}")
print(f"{'Revenue per MW':<45} {'$' + format(abilene_total_revenue/100, ',.0f'):>20} {'$' + format(houston_total_revenue/100, ',.0f'):>20}")
print(f"{'Weighted Avg Price Received ($/MWh)':<45} {'$' + format(abilene_wa_price, ',.2f'):>20} {'$' + format(houston_wa_price, ',.2f'):>20}")

abilene_all_avg = abilene_merged['lmp'].mean()
houston_all_avg = houston_merged['lmp'].mean()
print(f"{'Simple Avg RT LMP - All Hours ($/MWh)':<45} {'$' + format(abilene_all_avg, ',.2f'):>20} {'$' + format(houston_all_avg, ',.2f'):>20}")

diff = houston_total_revenue - abilene_total_revenue
pct_diff = diff / abilene_total_revenue * 100
print(f"{'Difference in Revenue (Houston - Abilene)':<45} {'':>20} {'$' + format(diff, ',.0f'):>20}")
print(f"{'% Difference':<45} {'':>20} {format(pct_diff, ',.1f') + '%':>20}")

print(f"\nMonthly Breakdown:")
print(f"\n{'Month':<8} {'Abilene Rev ($)':>18} {'Houston Rev ($)':>18} {'Abilene Gen (MWh)':>20} {'Houston Gen (MWh)':>20} {'Abilene Avg LMP':>16} {'Houston Avg LMP':>16}")
print("-" * 100)
months = ['Jan','Feb','Mar','Apr','May','Jun','Jul','Aug','Sep','Oct','Nov','Dec']
for i, row in abilene_monthly.iterrows():
    h_row = houston_monthly.iloc[i]
    print(f"{months[i]:<8} {row['revenue']:>18,.0f} {h_row['revenue']:>18,.0f} {row['generation']:>20,.0f} {h_row['generation']:>20,.0f} {row['avg_price']:>16,.2f} {h_row['avg_price']:>16,.2f}")

# Additional stats
print(f"\nAdditional Statistics:")
print(f"{'Max hourly revenue - Abilene':<45} {'$' + format(abilene_merged['revenue'].max(), ',.0f'):>20}")
print(f"{'Max hourly revenue - Houston':<45} {'$' + format(houston_merged['revenue'].max(), ',.0f'):>20}")
print(f"{'Hours with generation - Abilene':<45} {len(abilene_gen_hours):>20}")
print(f"{'Hours with generation - Houston':<45} {len(houston_gen_hours):>20}")
print(f"{'Hours with negative price during gen - Abilene':<45} {len(abilene_gen_hours[abilene_gen_hours['lmp']<0]):>20}")
print(f"{'Hours with negative price during gen - Houston':<45} {len(houston_gen_hours[houston_gen_hours['lmp']<0]):>20}")

# Revenue distribution by price tier
print(f"\nRevenue by Price Tier (during generation hours):")
tiers = [(-float('inf'), 0), (0, 25), (25, 50), (50, 100), (100, 250), (250, 1000), (1000, float('inf'))]
tier_labels = ['< $0', '$0-25', '$25-50', '$50-100', '$100-250', '$250-1000', '>$1000']
print(f"{'Tier ($/MWh)':<15} {'Abilene Rev ($)':>18} {'Abilene Gen (MWh)':>20} {'Houston Rev ($)':>18} {'Houston Gen (MWh)':>20}")
print("-" * 95)
for (lo, hi), label in zip(tiers, tier_labels):
    a_mask = (abilene_gen_hours['lmp'] >= lo) & (abilene_gen_hours['lmp'] < hi)
    h_mask = (houston_gen_hours['lmp'] >= lo) & (houston_gen_hours['lmp'] < hi)
    a_rev = abilene_gen_hours.loc[a_mask, 'revenue'].sum()
    a_gen = abilene_gen_hours.loc[a_mask, 'output_mw'].sum()
    h_rev = houston_gen_hours.loc[h_mask, 'revenue'].sum()
    h_gen = houston_gen_hours.loc[h_mask, 'output_mw'].sum()
    print(f"{label:<15} {a_rev:>18,.0f} {a_gen:>20,.0f} {h_rev:>18,.0f} {h_gen:>20,.0f}")

steps.content: Now let me create a visualization to accompany the analysis:

tool_input.code:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Quick summary of key findings for the final answer
print("KEY FINDINGS SUMMARY")
print("=" * 60)
print(f"Abilene (West Hub) Annual Revenue:   $22,085,152")
print(f"Houston (Houston Hub) Annual Revenue: $19,768,976")
print(f"Difference:                           -$2,316,176 (Abilene earns 10.5% more)")
print(f"")
print(f"Abilene Annual Generation:   219,720 MWh (CF: 25.08%)")
print(f"Houston Annual Generation:   193,193 MWh (CF: 22.05%)")
print(f"Generation Difference:       +26,527 MWh (Abilene generates 13.7% more)")
print(f"")
print(f"Abilene Weighted Avg Price:  $100.52/MWh")
print(f"Houston Weighted Avg Price:  $102.33/MWh")
print(f"Price Difference:            Houston receives $1.81/MWh more")
print(f"")
print(f"Abilene negative price hours during gen: 328")
print(f"Houston negative price hours during gen: 28")
print(f"")
print(f"August was the biggest revenue month for both:")
print(f"  Abilene:  $8,959,184 (40.6% of annual revenue)")
print(f"  Houston:  $8,836,770 (44.7% of annual revenue)")

steps.content:
---

# Comparison: 100 MW Solar PV Farm — Abilene (ERCOT West Hub) vs. Houston (ERCOT Houston Hub), 2023

## Setup & Assumptions

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| System Capacity | 100 MW |
| Tracking | Double-axis (2-axis) |
| System Loss | 10% |
| Curtailment | None assumed |
| Price Data | ERCOT Real-Time LMPs (15-min SCED, averaged to hourly) |
| Solar Generation | Renewables.ninja PV model (double-axis tracking, 10% system loss) |
| Year | 2023 (Jan 1 – Dec 31) |

**Sources:**
- Solar profiles: [Renewables.ninja](https://www.renewables.ninja/) PV generation model for Abilene, TX (32.45°N, 99.75°W) and Houston, TX (29.76°N, 95.37°W)
- Real-time electricity prices: ERCOT LMP data from the `ercot_db.ercot_lmp` table (zone_name = 'west' and 'houston', is_day_ahead = false)

---

## Headline Results

| Metric | Abilene (West Hub) | Houston (Houston Hub) | Difference |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| **Annual Generation (MWh)** | 219,720 | 193,193 | +26,527 (Abilene +13.7%) |
| **Capacity Factor** | 25.08% | 22.05% | +3.03 pp |
| **Annual Real-Time Revenue** | **$22,085,152** | **$19,768,976** | **+$2,316,176 (Abilene +10.5%)** |
| **Revenue per MW** | $220,852 | $197,690 | +$23,162 |
| **Weighted Avg Price Received ($/MWh)** | $100.52 | $102.33 | –$1.81 (Houston slightly higher) |
| **Simple Avg RT LMP – All Hours ($/MWh)** | $54.72 | $50.01 | +$4.71 |
| **Hours with Negative Prices During Generation** | 328 | 28 | +300 (Abilene far more) |

---

## Key Takeaways

### 1. Abilene Wins on Revenue Despite Lower Prices
Abilene generates **$2.3 million more** in annual real-time revenue (+10.5%) than Houston. This is driven entirely by **higher energy output** — Abilene's superior solar resource (more sunny days, higher irradiance in West Texas) yields 13.7% more generation (219,720 MWh vs. 193,193 MWh). Even though Houston receives a marginally higher weighted-average price ($102.33/MWh vs. $100.52/MWh), the generation advantage more than compensates.

### 2. Houston Has Better Price Capture
Houston's weighted-average price received during solar hours ($102.33/MWh) is slightly higher than Abilene's ($100.52/MWh). This is because the **West Hub experiences far more negative price hours during solar generation** (328 hours vs. only 28 in Houston), reflecting the heavy solar congestion in West Texas. Houston's load center proximity means less solar-driven price suppression.

### 3. August Dominates Revenue for Both Locations
August 2023 was an extraordinary revenue month due to extreme heat and price spikes across ERCOT:

| Month | Abilene Revenue | Houston Revenue | Abilene Gen (MWh) | Houston Gen (MWh) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Jan | $505,862 | $397,219 | 16,385 | 14,253 |
| Feb | $316,114 | $279,904 | 14,809 | 14,071 |
| Mar | $803,118 | $598,156 | 19,001 | 16,135 |
| Apr | $727,754 | $441,330 | 20,842 | 15,870 |
| May | $872,604 | $662,183 | 16,213 | 15,231 |
| Jun | $2,460,516 | $2,136,575 | 20,017 | 17,902 |
| Jul | $1,541,319 | $1,479,847 | 21,922 | 19,108 |
| **Aug** | **$8,959,184** | **$8,836,770** | 21,661 | 18,642 |
| Sep | $3,650,657 | $3,370,817 | 18,317 | 16,988 |
| Oct | $831,567 | $707,812 | 16,811 | 16,435 |
| Nov | $736,671 | $497,833 | 16,568 | 13,541 |
| Dec | $679,784 | $360,529 | 17,173 | 15,017 |

August alone accounted for **40.6%** of Abilene's annual revenue and **44.7%** of Houston's. This reflects the extreme pricing events during the August 2023 heat wave, when ERCOT real-time prices spiked above $5,000/MWh.

### 4. Revenue by Price Tier

| Price Tier ($/MWh) | Abilene Revenue | Abilene Gen (MWh) | Houston Revenue | Houston Gen (MWh) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| < $0 (negative) | –$58,281 | 15,056 | –$4,294 | 1,092 |
| $0–25 | $1,034,619 | 71,660 | $1,501,773 | 82,260 |
| $25–50 | $2,400,346 | 68,065 | $2,201,313 | 66,037 |
| $50–100 | $2,703,597 | 38,918 | $1,580,625 | 23,473 |
| $100–250 | $2,072,321 | 14,567 | $1,520,888 | 10,287 |
| $250–1,000 | $3,636,858 | 7,150 | $2,987,686 | 5,827 |
| >$1,000 | $10,295,692 | 4,304 | $9,980,984 | 4,217 |

The **>$1,000/MWh tier** alone generated ~$10 million for both locations — nearly half of total annual revenue — from just ~4,300 MWh of generation during extreme price events. Abilene lost $58,281 to negative pricing (328 hours), while Houston lost only $4,294 (28 hours).

---

## Conclusion

A 100 MW double-axis tracking solar farm in **Abilene, TX (ERCOT West Hub)** would have earned approximately **$22.1 million** in real-time energy revenue in 2023, compared to **$19.8 million** for an identical farm in **Houston, TX (ERCOT Houston Hub)** — a **$2.3 million (10.5%) advantage** for Abilene. The revenue advantage is entirely driven by Abilene's superior solar resource (25.1% vs. 22.1% capacity factor), which more than offsets the West Hub's greater exposure to negative pricing during solar hours. However, Houston offers slightly better price capture per MWh and far fewer negative-price hours, suggesting that if curtailment or congestion were factored in, the gap could narrow. Both locations are heavily dependent on extreme pricing events — August 2023 alone contributed over 40% of annual revenue for both sites.
